game_description: Race your checkers home and bear them off before your opponent, 2 players.
filter_1_label: Ahead in pip count (closer to home)?

strategies.strategy_detailed: Bot-confirmed best plays: 3-1 makes the 5-point (8/5, 6/5), 4-2 makes the 4-point (8/4, 6/4), 6-1 makes the bar-point (13/7, 8/7), 5-3 makes the 3-point (8/3, 6/3). 6-5 runs a back checker (24/13, lover's leap). Pointing beats splitting on these rolls — XG agrees unanimously.

After the opening exchange, compute pip counts. Lead by 8%+ and clear of contact → race. Roughly even with contact → priming/holding game. Far behind with deep anchors → back game. Pip count is the single most important metric for plan selection — recompute every few rolls.
strategy_stealth: Recompute pip count every few rolls

strategies.condition: Building an anchor in opponent home
strategies.strategy_detailed: The 20-point (opponent's 5-point) is the golden anchor — high enough to re-enter fast, deep enough to threaten hits during opponent's bear-off. The 21-point (bar anchor) is second best. Avoid sticking on the 24-point: it dies to a 6-prime and you lose initiative.

strategies.condition: Opportunity to hit an opponent blot
strategies.strategy_detailed: Hit when you don't leave a return shot you can't cover. Home-board hits are nearly always correct — opponent loses tempo plus risks closeout. Outer-board hits need a follow-up plan (build a point next turn, or hit again). Pure-tempo hits with no positional gain are usually wrong.
strategy_stealth: Hit if no uncovered return shot

strategies.strategy_detailed: A 6-prime (six consecutive closed points) traps opponent checkers — no roll escapes. Build from the 5/6-point backward to the bar (7) and 4-point. A 5-prime is strong but escapable on the exact gap-matching roll. Maintain prime integrity while bringing back checkers around it.

In a pure race, minimize wastage: aim for 2 checkers per home-board point. Excess checkers on the 6-point waste pips when smaller rolls bear off. With no contact, smooth distribution can be worth 4-8 pips versus heavy stacking — often the margin between win and loss.

Cube theory: take point ~25% (accept if you win 25%+ for money play). Initial double window opens at ~50% in volatile positions, ~65-70% in races. Above ~80% you're 'too good' — play on for gammon. Match play shifts these thresholds; consult a match equity table.

While opponent dances on the bar, race to close home points. With 5 of 6 home points closed, opponent dances 25/36 rolls (enters only on the 11/36 with a matching die). Closeout = automatic dance. Slot and cover aggressively — hits while building are usually correct.

strategies.condition: Behind in pips with 2+ deep anchors
strategies.strategy_detailed: Best back-game anchors: 2 and 3-point, or 1 and 3-point. Wait for opponent to leave shots during bear-off and hit into your closed inner board. Critical: manage timing — keep mid-board checkers active so you don't crunch (forced to break anchors) before the shot appears.

strategies.condition: Bearing off with all checkers home
strategies.strategy_detailed: If opponent holds a deep anchor, bear off safely: never leave a direct shot to their anchor unless forced. With no contact, use high rolls on high points, lower rolls to fill gaps or move down. Single checkers on 5/6-points late are blot risks if opponent breaks anchor to attack.

A gammon doubles the loss (backgammon triples). If you haven't borne any off and opponent is mid-bear-off, prioritize getting one checker off over positioning. Break anchors only if doing so gives you a real shot or speeds bear-off — premature breaks cost gammons, not save them.
